SAS vs. SPSS: Playing with the big guns

Steve Doig The Miami Herald April 1996
Data & DatabasesInvestigative MethodsGovernment & Politics

If you can touch-type your way around a spreadsheet, and you speak fluent SQL to your database program, it's time to add a new power tool to your CARpentry workbench: One of the major statistical anal...

If you can touch-type your way around a spreadsheet, and you speak fluent SQL to your database program, it's time to add a new power tool to your CARpentry workbench: One of the major statistical analysis software packages, either SAS or SPSS. Here's where I'd like to tell you that, despite what you'd heard, SAS and/or SPSS are cheap and easy to master. I'd like to, but I'd be telling a lie. Actually, they're damned expensive, and they can do things you'll have trouble pronouncing (say "partial canonical correlation analysis" three times), much less readily understand how to use.

... In sum, using SAS or SPSS for routine CAR problems may seem a little like using a construction crane to plant flowerpots. But when the day comes that you need to do some really heavy data-lifting, you'll be glad you have either one.
